content.text: Feasibility gates enforce a risk-gated funding model for agentic AI programs, requiring each initiative to pass structured checkpoints across technical, compliance, business, and operational risk dimensions before capital is committed. By making go/no-go decisions explicit and criterion-based, organisations avoid the most expensive outcome in AI projects: funding a build that should have been killed at design time.

attrs.alt: Risk-based feasibility gate framework showing four sequential gates (Technical, Compliance, Business, Operational) before funding authorization

content.text: What It Is

A risk-based feasibility gate is a formal decision point in a project lifecycle where a defined set of risk criteria must be evaluated and resolved before the project is permitted to advance to the next stage or receive allocated budget. For agentic AI programmes, gates are structured around the four principal risk categories: technical feasibility, regulatory and compliance fit, business viability, and operational readiness.

Each gate produces one of three outcomes: PASS (proceed), HOLD (proceed only after specified conditions are met), or KILL (discontinue and capture learnings). Gates are not approvals in a bureaucratic sense — they are risk-weighted evidence reviews. The evidence required at each gate is defined upfront during programme governance design, making the criteria objective rather than political.

Feasibility gates differ from general programme milestones in that they are explicitly authorised to stop a project. A milestone tracks whether work happened; a gate determines whether it should continue. This distinction is crucial: organisations that treat gates as rubber stamps consistently over-invest in unviable AI initiatives.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI projects carry compounded risk compared with conventional software: they require fine-grained data access, make autonomous decisions, interact with external systems, and are difficult to audit post-hoc. The cost of discovering a fatal flaw at sprint 8 — after four months of engineering and integration work — dwarfs the cost of surfacing the same flaw at a pre-funding gate review lasting two days.

Common failure patterns that feasibility gates catch early include: data that exists in the organisation but cannot be legally shared with an LLM (compliance gate); a latency SLA incompatible with multi-step agent reasoning (technical gate); an ROI case dependent on a headcount reduction that HR has not approved (business gate); and an agent that modifies production records with no rollback procedure designed (operational gate). Each of these would produce a project failure after significant spend if not caught before funding.

Beyond individual project risk, feasibility gates create institutional knowledge. Kill decisions, when documented, build a library of constraint patterns that pre-screens future proposals before they reach the gate — accelerating programme portfolio throughput over time.
Benefits
Capital protection: funding is not released until evidence satisfies each gate criterion, preventing investment in unviable projects.
Early risk discovery: structured criteria surface compliance and operational blockers during discovery rather than during integration testing.
Stakeholder alignment: gate reviews create a shared understanding of what risks exist and who owns each mitigation, reducing post-launch blame cycles.
Portfolio prioritisation: programmes rated across the same risk dimensions are directly comparable, allowing a portfolio committee to rank competing initiatives objectively.
Regulatory defensibility: documented gate evidence is audit-ready evidence that due diligence was performed before deploying an automated decision system.
Reduced wasted engineering: kill decisions at gate 1 or 2 cost hours of review time; the same kill at sprint 10 costs months of engineering time.
Trade-offs

Gates add process overhead. Well-designed gates consume two to five person-days of evidence gathering and review per project. For a portfolio of 20 concurrent programmes, this is non-trivial. Poorly designed gates with vague criteria add overhead without adding risk signal, creating the worst combination: slow and uninformative.

Gates can also suppress genuinely innovative proposals that cannot yet produce polished evidence packages. Experimental initiatives — where the point is to learn whether something is feasible — should use lighter-weight exploration gates rather than full funding gates. Applying full-weight criteria to a research spike mismatch the gate to the risk profile.

Finally, gates do not eliminate political pressure. A strong executive sponsor can push a HOLD to PASS without meeting criteria. Gate frameworks must have explicit escalation policies and document override events — otherwise the gate is a paper exercise.
How It Works

Each gate consists of a criterion set, a weighting scheme, an evidence checklist, and a threshold for each possible outcome. Before the gate review meeting, a programme analyst collects the required evidence artefacts: data lineage maps, legal review memos, cost model spreadsheets, runbook drafts, and so on. These are scored against each criterion on a standardised scale.

A common scoring approach uses a three-level rating per criterion: Green (criterion satisfied, no caveats), Yellow (criterion partially satisfied, named condition required), or Red (criterion not satisfied, no known path to satisfaction). Overall gate outcome is determined by the distribution of reds, yellows, and greens: any red in a blocking criterion is an automatic Kill; three or more yellows in a single gate is a Hold; all greens or one yellow is a Pass.

Blocking criteria are designated upfront by the gate framework designer. For Gate 2 (Compliance), a data-residency violation or a missing legal basis for processing is typically blocking — no mitigation can clear it within the project timeline, so the project must be redesigned or killed. For Gate 1 (Technical), a high latency estimate is usually a Hold, not a Kill, because architectural changes (caching, streaming, model switching) can address it.

Gate 1 — Technical Risk — evaluates whether the engineering approach is sound. It scores data availability, API and tool access permissions, end-to-end latency budget (P95 agent call chain), model capability for the required task type, and integration depth with downstream systems. The core question is: given current constraints, can a working system be built at all?

Gate 2 — Compliance Risk — evaluates regulatory and legal fit. Criteria include PII/PCI data exposure in agent context, classification under the EU AI Act or equivalent regulation, audit trail requirements that the agent architecture must satisfy, data residency constraints for model API calls, and validity of the data processing consent chain. A project that requires sending customer health records to a US-hosted LLM endpoint while subject to a European data residency obligation fails Gate 2 immediately.

Gate 3 — Business Risk — evaluates commercial viability. Criteria include ROI confidence level (is the case based on measured baselines or assumptions?), scope creep risk (is the agent boundary well-defined?), dependency count on third-party systems outside the team's control, build versus buy analysis, and organisational change readiness score from the stakeholder map. A project with nine external dependencies and no change champion network fails on dependency count and change readiness simultaneously.

Gate 4 — Operational Risk — evaluates production sustainability. Criteria include available on-call capacity and skills, the completeness and testability of the rollback plan, blast radius if the agent fails open (takes wrong autonomous actions at scale), the existence of an incident runbook written for non-ML engineers, and the feasibility of a safe degraded fallback mode. A project whose blast radius includes modifying live financial records without human review fails Gate 4 regardless of other scores.
Architecture

The diagram above shows the sequential gate structure. Each gate is independently scored and can terminate the project. The KILL path exits downward from each gate with a requirement to document learnings before closing the project. The path to FUNDED status requires all four gates to return PASS or conditional PASS (HOLD with conditions explicitly resolved). The sequence is always G1 → G2 → G3 → G4; gates are not run in parallel because later gates often depend on the resolved outputs of earlier ones (G2 compliance review needs the G1 data access findings).
When to Use It

Full four-gate reviews apply to projects above a defined investment threshold (typically 50K–00K build cost), projects that involve autonomous decision-making on behalf of customers or employees, projects with access to sensitive data categories (PII, financial, health, legal), and projects that modify external state (write to databases, send communications, trigger financial transactions).

Lightweight two-gate reviews (Technical + Compliance only) apply to internal tooling agents with limited blast radius, proof-of-concept spikes with a defined kill date, and agent features added to already-approved systems that do not change the data access or risk profile.

Gates are not appropriate for pure research experiments with no path to production, for iterative model evaluations within an already-funded programme, or for agent configuration changes that do not alter scope or risk profile. Applying gates to every minor change creates review fatigue and erodes their signal value.
How to Implement

Implementing a risk-based feasibility gate framework involves four design phases: defining the criterion sets, calibrating the thresholds, building the evidence templates, and establishing the governance structure.
Step 1: Define Criterion Sets

For each gate, list the specific risk questions the organisation needs answered. Criteria should be answerable with a Green/Yellow/Red score from a concrete evidence artefact. Avoid vague criteria like 'technical approach is sound' — replace with measurable equivalents like 'P95 end-to-end agent latency is under 4 seconds in load testing' or 'all required APIs are accessible from the deployment VPC without firewall changes'.
Step 2: Mark Blocking Criteria

Identify which criteria, if Red, are an automatic Kill regardless of other scores. These should be criteria where no realistic mitigation exists within the programme timeline. Common blocking criteria: missing legal basis for data processing (G2), no fallback mode for a safety-critical workflow (G4), model accuracy below the threshold required for the use case even after fine-tuning (G1).
Step 3: Build Evidence Templates

For each criterion, specify the artefact that satisfies it. This prevents ambiguity in gate preparation and makes gate reviews consistent across reviewers. Evidence for G2 PII exposure might be: a data flow diagram annotated with PII fields, signed off by the DPO. Evidence for G4 rollback plan might be: a tested runbook with a named owner and a recovery time objective.
Step 4: Establish Governance

Each gate should have a gate owner (the person who signs off the outcome), a reviewer panel (cross-functional, typically 3–5 people), and an override policy. Override events — where a gate outcome is changed without meeting criteria — must be documented with the approver name and the stated justification, and reviewed quarterly. An override frequency above 10% of gates is a signal that either the criteria are wrong or the governance is not functioning.
Examples
Example 1: Gate Scoring Dataclasses
Example 2: Full Gate Review Pipeline

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class GateReviewRecord:
    gate: Gate
    outcome: GateOutcome
    reasons: list[str]
    reviewer: str
    review_date: date
    override: bool = False
    override_justification: str = ""


class FeasibilityGatePipeline:
    """Runs all four gates in sequence, short-circuiting on Kill."""

    def __init__(self, project_name: str):
        self.project_name = project_name
        self.records: list[GateReviewRecord] = []

    def run_gate(
        self,
        gate: Gate,
        reviewer: str,
        review_date: date,
        override: bool = False,
        override_justification: str = "",
    ) -> GateReviewRecord:
        if self.is_killed():
            raise RuntimeError(f"Project '{self.project_name}' was already killed")

        outcome, reasons = gate.evaluate()

        # Apply override (must document justification)
        if override and outcome == GateOutcome.KILL:
            if not override_justification:
                raise ValueError("override_justification is required when overriding a Kill")
            outcome = GateOutcome.HOLD
            reasons.append(f"Override applied by {reviewer}: {override_justification}")

        record = GateReviewRecord(
            gate=gate,
            outcome=outcome,
            reasons=reasons,
            reviewer=reviewer,
            review_date=review_date,
            override=override,
            override_justification=override_justification,
        )
        self.records.append(record)
        return record

    def is_killed(self) -> bool:
        return any(r.outcome == GateOutcome.KILL for r in self.records)

    def is_funded(self) -> bool:
        if self.is_killed():
            return False
        if len(self.records) < 4:
            return False
        return all(r.outcome in (GateOutcome.PASS, GateOutcome.HOLD) for r in self.records)

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"=== {self.project_name} Gate Summary ==="]
        for i, r in enumerate(self.records, 1):
            lines.append(f"  Gate {i} ({r.gate.name}): {r.outcome.value.upper()}")
            for reason in r.reasons:
                lines.append(f"    - {reason}")
        if self.is_funded():
            lines.append("STATUS: FUNDED — sprint-0 authorised")
        elif self.is_killed():
            lines.append("STATUS: KILLED — document learnings before closing")
        else:
            lines.append(f"STATUS: IN REVIEW — {4 - len(self.records)} gate(s) remaining")
        return "
".join(lines)


# Usage example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    project = FeasibilityGatePipeline("Customer FAQ Agent")

    gate1 = Gate("Technical Risk", criteria=[
        Criterion("Data availability", "FAQ KB accessible from agent runtime", Score.GREEN, blocking=False),
        Criterion("API access", "Zendesk API keys provisioned", Score.GREEN, blocking=False),
        Criterion("Latency", "P95 < 4s in staging", Score.YELLOW,
                  hold_condition="Run load test with 200 concurrent users before sprint-2", blocking=False),
    ])
    record1 = project.run_gate(gate1, reviewer="alice@example.com", review_date=date(2025, 9, 1))
    print(record1.outcome)  # PASS (one yellow, under threshold)

    gate2 = Gate("Compliance Risk", criteria=[
        Criterion("PII exposure", "No customer PII in FAQ corpus", Score.GREEN, blocking=True),
        Criterion("AI Act tier", "Classified as limited-risk, disclosure required", Score.YELLOW,
                  hold_condition="Add 'AI-assisted' label to agent responses by go-live", blocking=False),
        Criterion("Data residency", "Model API calls routed through EU endpoint", Score.GREEN, blocking=True),
    ])
    record2 = project.run_gate(gate2, reviewer="bob@example.com", review_date=date(2025, 9, 3))
    print(record2.outcome)  # PASS

    print(project.summary())
Real-World Example

A financial services firm running a loan underwriting agent programme used a four-gate framework before committing 00K in build budget. Gate 1 surfaced that the credit bureau API required a dedicated egress IP address not available in the shared cloud environment — a three-week infrastructure change converted this from Yellow to Green. Gate 2 flagged that the agent's use of customer income data from a bank-aggregation API required a new legal basis under GDPR Article 6 — a Kill criterion that triggered a legal design change to use bank-verified payroll data instead, restructuring the data pipeline entirely.

Gate 3 revealed that the ROI model assumed a 70% automation rate but the only comparable deployment in the industry ran at 40% — the ROI case was restructured around the conservative figure, with a success criterion of reaching 55% within 12 months of go-live before the next investment tranche. Gate 4 found no written rollback procedure and no testing of the fallback (manual underwriting queue) under simulated agent failure — a two-week runbook authoring exercise resolved this.

Total gate review time: 12 person-days across all four gates. Time to resolve all Hold conditions: 6 weeks. Without the gates, the legal data issue would have been discovered during UAT at week 14, requiring a six-month architecture redesign after significant engineering spend. The gate framework saved an estimated 00K in wasted engineering.
Common Mistakes
Vague Criteria That Cannot Be Scored

Criteria like 'the team has adequate skills' or 'the business case is compelling' are subjective and produce inconsistent scoring across reviewers. Replace with: 'at least one team member has production experience with the specific model family being deployed' and 'the NPV at a 12% discount rate is positive under the conservative scenario'.
Running Gates as Approval Ceremonies

When gates become a formality that approves every project regardless of scores, they provide false assurance. Teams stop taking the evidence gathering seriously, criterion scores drift to Green by default, and the gate consumes time without producing signal. Quarterly audits of gate scores versus actual project outcomes catch this pattern.
Skipping Gates for 'Fast-Track' Projects

Executive pressure to skip gates 'just this once' for a high-priority initiative is the most common failure mode. Fast-track projects are high-priority precisely because they are high-stakes — which means they need more scrutiny, not less. A lightweight version of each gate is appropriate; eliminating gates entirely is not.
No Kill Decision Authority

Gate reviewers who lack authority to issue a Kill are not gate reviewers — they are advisors. If the programme sponsor can override any Kill without documentation or escalation, the gate framework is purely theatre. The governance design must specify who has kill authority and what the override process costs in terms of accountability.
Best Practices
Pre-screen with a one-page risk brief before scheduling a full gate review — this filters obviously unviable proposals before consuming reviewer time.
Publish gate criteria publicly within the organisation so programme teams can self-assess before formal submission, reducing surprises at the gate meeting.
Time-box gate reviews: two hours for gates 1 and 2, two hours for gates 3 and 4. Evidence packets are submitted 48 hours in advance so reviewers arrive prepared.
Record Hold conditions as named, owned, time-bound commitments — not as vague intentions. A HOLD condition without an owner and a due date is a deferred Kill.
Maintain a kill log with documented reasons; review quarterly to identify recurring failure patterns that indicate systemic gaps in the proposal process.

Calibrate gates periodically: compare gate predictions against actual project outcomes after 12 months. If projects that passed Gate 3 consistently fail on the business case, the ROI criteria need tightening.
Performance and Scalability

Gate framework throughput is bounded by reviewer bandwidth. A panel of five reviewers, each with two hours per gate review, can support roughly two to three full programme reviews per week. At 20 concurrent programmes, this creates a bottleneck. Solutions include: tiered gate depth (full four-gate for >00K, two-gate for 0K–00K, self-service checklist for <0K); asynchronous evidence review followed by a one-hour decision meeting; and rotating reviewer pools that spread review load across domain experts.

A common scalability pattern is to run gates 1 and 2 (Technical + Compliance) as a pre-screening pair that eliminates non-viable proposals before spending review bandwidth on gates 3 and 4. Empirically, 30–40% of proposals fail gates 1 or 2, so only 60–70% of proposals consume the full four-gate cycle.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Gate frameworks are most commonly implemented in three ways: as structured documents in Confluence or Notion (low-tech, accessible, hard to report on at portfolio scale); as structured form submissions in Jira with a custom workflow enforcing sequential gate states (medium complexity, integrates with existing project tracking); or as dedicated governance platforms such as Planview, Templeton, or Archer GRC (full portfolio visibility, automated scoring aggregation, audit trail).

# Lightweight gate tracking using a simple JSON state file
# Suitable for small teams before a full GRC tool is warranted

import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date


def load_gate_state(project_id: str, state_dir: Path) -> dict:
    path = state_dir / f"{project_id}_gates.json"
    if path.exists():
        return json.loads(path.read_text())
    return {"project_id": project_id, "gates": {}, "status": "pending"}


def record_gate_outcome(
    project_id: str,
    gate_name: str,
    outcome: str,
    reviewer: str,
    reasons: list[str],
    state_dir: Path,
) -> dict:
    state = load_gate_state(project_id, state_dir)
    state["gates"][gate_name] = {
        "outcome": outcome,
        "reviewer": reviewer,
        "date": date.today().isoformat(),
        "reasons": reasons,
    }
    # Derive overall status
    if any(g["outcome"] == "kill" for g in state["gates"].values()):
        state["status"] = "killed"
    elif len(state["gates"]) == 4 and all(
        g["outcome"] in ("pass", "hold") for g in state["gates"].values()
    ):
        state["status"] = "funded"
    path = state_dir / f"{project_id}_gates.json"
    path.write_text(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
    return state


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import tempfile
    d = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
    state = record_gate_outcome(
        "proj-faq-agent", "technical", "pass",
        "alice@example.com", ["All GREEN"], d
    )
    state = record_gate_outcome(
        "proj-faq-agent", "compliance", "hold",
        "bob@example.com",
        ["Condition: add AI disclosure label before go-live"], d
    )
    print(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Decision Model
Risk Visibility
Audit Trail
Best For
Risk-Based Gates (this)
Sequential, criterion-scored, kill authority
High — four explicit dimensions
Strong — per-criterion evidence records
High-stakes, regulated, autonomous agents
Stage-Gate (Cooper)
Sequential stages, business deliverables
Medium — business focus, less technical
Good — stage deliverables are documented
Product development, NPD programmes
Agile Sprint Review
Iterative, outcome-based, no hard kill
Low — risk implicit in backlog priorities
Weak — velocity-focused, not risk-focused
Low-stakes iterative feature development
Investment Committee (ad hoc)
Discretionary, relationship-driven
Variable — depends on committee expertise
Weak — decisions often undocumented
Small teams, early-stage exploration
Risk Register Review
Parallel risk tracking, no funding control
High — detailed risk log
Strong
Ongoing monitoring, not pre-funding
Security and Reliability

Gate frameworks protect against a specific class of reliability failure: funding and deploying an agent system without adequate risk vetting. The compliance gate (G2) is the primary mechanism for catching security-relevant risks before build. Criteria to always include: model API call data classification (does the agent send data classified above the model provider's permitted data tier?), prompt injection attack surface (does the agent accept untrusted user input that flows into tool call parameters?), and access control model (does the agent's service account follow least privilege?).

The operational gate (G4) covers reliability: does the blast radius of agent failure include irreversible actions? Is there a circuit breaker that pauses the agent if error rates exceed a threshold? Is the on-call rotation prepared to handle agent-specific failure modes that differ from conventional software failures? These questions are not answered at the gate — they are confirmed to have been answered with evidence. The distinction is important: the gate is not the risk review; it is the verification that the risk review happened and its outputs were acted upon.
Mental Model

Think of risk-based feasibility gates as the building inspection system for agentic AI. A building inspector does not design the building — they verify, at defined checkpoints, that the design and construction meet minimum safety standards before the structure is occupied. An inspector who always passes buildings regardless of findings provides false assurance. An inspector who has no authority to halt construction cannot protect future occupants. Feasibility gates work the same way: they are independent, criterion-based checkpoints with real authority to halt investment, protecting the organisation from occupying (deploying) a structure (agent system) that is not safe to use.
Key Takeaways
Risk-based feasibility gates enforce four sequential checkpoints — technical, compliance, business, and operational — before funding is released to an agentic AI programme.
Each gate produces PASS, HOLD, or KILL; gates have genuine kill authority — without it, the framework is a formality that provides false assurance.
Blocking criteria are defined upfront: any Red on a blocking criterion is an automatic Kill regardless of other scores.
HOLD outcomes require named, owned, time-bound conditions; an unowned HOLD condition is a deferred Kill.
Gate criteria must be measurable and evidence-based — vague criteria produce inconsistent scoring and erode reviewer confidence in the framework.
Override events must be documented with approver name and justification; override frequency above 10% is a governance health warning.
Calibrate gate criteria annually against actual project outcomes to ensure predictive validity — gates that do not predict failure are not functioning as designed.
